title: Spin SWAP operation in double quantum dots at the LaAlO3/SrTiO3 interface
authors: A. Sierant, J. Czarnecki, B. Szafran, P. Wójcik
abstract: Progress in the fabrication of nanoscale transition-metal-oxide heterostructures makes these platforms promising candidates for the realization of spin qubits, mainly due to the $d$-character of their electronic structures, which could potentially result in a reduction of hyperfine interactions and spin decoherence. Here, we present a systematic study of spin control within the SWAP operation in double quantum dots embedded in a two-dimensional electron gas at the LaAlO$_3$/SrTiO$_3$ interface. Our analysis starts with a study of single-electron spin dynamics, focusing on the influence of spin-orbit and interorbital coupling on the spin evolution. In this case, our findings are supported by semiclassical calculations based on the Bloch equations, which show good agreement with full quantum mechanical simulations. We then simulate the SWAP operation by analyzing the crossover between two regimes: (i) large quantum dots, where the electronic structure is dominated by the $d_{xy}$ orbitals and the spin dynamics is affected primarily by Rashba-type spin-orbit interaction; and (ii) small quantum dots, where higher-energy orbitals $d_{xz/yz}$ contribute to the electronic structure, leading to a significant reduction in the SWAP fidelity. In the first regime, particularly relevant from the application point of view, we analyze in detail the anisotropy of the SWAP operation induced by the spin-orbit coupling.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates the feasibility of implementing a robust SWAP gate for spin qubits in double quantum dots formed at the LaAlO3/SrTiO3 interface. The authors analyze how varying the dot size and leveraging orbital physics (d-character) allows for control over spin dynamics, showing that SO coupling introduces both opportunities (anisotropy) and challenges (fidelity reduction). It provides crucial guidelines for designing next-generation solid-state quantum memories.
main_problem: The study investigates spin control during the SWAP operation in double quantum dots (QDs) fabricated at the LaAlO3/SrTiO3 interface, focusing on how multi-orbital character and spin-orbit coupling affect fidelity.
main_result: The SWAP time varies significantly with QD size, and high fidelity requires operating in an intermediate regime where SO interaction is controlled relative to kinetic energy. Furthermore, the anisotropy of the operation can be exploited by aligning initial polarization along specific axes.
method: The analysis employs a combination of full quantum mechanical simulations (solving the two-electron Hamiltonian) and semi-classical approaches using Bloch equations to model spin dynamics over time.
model_or_system: The system is double QDs embedded in a 2DEG at the LaAlO3/SrTiO3 interface, modeled using a tight-binding Hamiltonian incorporating kinetic energy, atomic SO coupling, Rashba interaction, and electron-electron Coulomb repulsion. The physics spans two regimes: large dots (Rashba-dominated) and small dots (higher orbital contributions).
key_observables: SWAP operation fidelity, spin expectation values ($\langle S_x angle, \langle S_y angle, \langle S_z angle$), and the exchange energy ($J$).
important_parameters: QD diameter (d), SO coupling strengths ($\Delta_{	ext{SO}}, \Delta_{	ext{RSO}}$), and confinement potential parameters.
main_assumptions: The semi-classical Bloch approach assumes separation of spin and positional degrees of freedom, which is noted to break down in certain regimes. Effective Hamiltonian derivations involve approximations regarding the largest energy scale ($\Delta E$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate confining potentials, time evolution of single/two-electron spin expectation values showing precession or beating patterns, and plots detailing the exchange energy ($J$) versus QD size or barrier height.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes single-electron dynamics first (using Bloch equations), then moves to two-electron spin control via the SWAP operation. It contrasts results across different QD sizes (large vs. small) and details how SO coupling introduces anisotropy and affects fidelity by mixing orbital character ($d_{xy}$ vs $d_{xz}/d_{yz}$).
why_interesting: This work directly addresses solid-state quantum computation platforms, providing detailed theoretical predictions on controlling spin qubits using engineered material interfaces (STO/LAO) where strong spin-orbit coupling is inherent.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The confining potential in the double LAO/STO QD for the following set of parameters: µ = 6, V0 = 10 meV, Vb = 10 meV, and Rb = 43a.

FIG. 3. Time evolution of the expectation value of the spin in the left (solid lines) and right (dashed lines) dots. The x, y, and z components of the spin are marked in blue, red, and black, respectively. Panels [(d)–(f)] show the average electron position x as a function of time. Results for a quantum dot size of d = 5.5 nm.

FIG. 2. Single electron spectrum of double LAO/STO QD as a function of quantum dot size d. Contributions from different orbitals dxy, dxz and dyz are marked using an RGB color scheme. Results for the confining potential parameters Vb = 70 meV, V0 = 10 meV and Rb = 70 meV.
FIG. 4. Same as Fig. 3, but for a quantum dot size of d = 33 nm.

FIG. 5. The expectation value of spin from the full quan- tum mechanical calulations presented in Fig. 4 (solid lines) compared with the solution of the Bloch equations with a Rashba-like SO coupling, Eq. (23) (indicated by diamonds). Results for d = 33 nm.
